input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements, operating within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). The system is characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) necessitating computational offloading for critical operations, real-time data monitoring capabilities, and safety-critical functionality requiring low-latency communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) that displays real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, with capabilities to save and generate reports; (2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system's lead vehicle detection, desired speed computation, and actuation control are offloaded to cloud-based resources to alleviate on-board hardware constraints; and (3) Remote Management enabling operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking commands remotely. Target users include operators for real-time monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using collected data.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Hardware components include NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, and Vedder Electronic Speed Controller. Software tools comprise NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) middleware, Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for simulation and development.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms is required for coordination between devices to enable computation offloading, as constrained by hardware limitations; this is less stringent than safety-critical vehicular standards which mandate synchronization better than 100 ms.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Decentralized architecture with three distinct entities (User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, Physical Twin Entity) deployed on separate hardware components. Physical Twin Entity handles resource-specific functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Digital Twin Entity manages data processing and control mechanisms, and User Entity includes split components (3D Visualization and Control Panel) deviating from ISO 23247 RA by redefining Physical Twin Entity's role and functionality ownership.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: ISO 23247 RA-based architecture mapping software components to Functional Entities implemented as ROS2 packages containing ROS2 nodes. Entities include User Entity (3D Visualization, Control Panel), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and Physical Twin Entity (resource-specific functions like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), which replaces the standard OME, impacting functionality ownership and engineering processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking implemented with DBSCAN algorithm and Kalman filter for object trajectory prediction, and an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) component for vehicle speed regulation. Data management utilizes a SQL database for structured storage and Data Collection modules for real-time processing of object tracks and vehicle state parameters including speed and steering angle.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw sensor data from physical components (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor, steering servo) is transmitted to the Data Collecting FE via Wi-Fi using UDP transport protocol. The Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component processes raw LiDAR data into object tracks, followed by serialization, message type alignment, and unit conversion via the ISO 23247 Data Translation component. Real-time ACC-relevant data including vehicle state, object tracks, and projected driving path are integrated into the 3D Visualization system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the ACC Controlling FE are serialized and translated by the Data Translation component before transmission over Wi-Fi using UDP to physical actuators (Motor and Steering Servo). The document does not specify command validation, authentication, or feedback mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Intra-digital twin software component communication uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication between physical and digital entities occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP transport layer protocol. Bandwidth, latency, redundancy, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin provides decision support through services including Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management. The user interface enables viewing, saving, generating reports, and remote management of the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 standard, which includes four parts: Overview and general principles, Reference architecture (Part 2), Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements (Part 3), and Information exchange (Part 4). The reference architecture defines entities such as Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Information exchange standards specify interoperability protocols, with OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) cited as a relevant technology for data collection and device control implementation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle' with 'lower and upper chassis layers' and 'autonomy elements', directly supported by Figure 1 and the text describing the 'physical architecture consists of three layers'. It correctly identifies the operational domain within 'Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)' and 'Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)', as stated in the abstract and introduction. The mention of 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs)' necessitating 'computational offloading' is directly supported by the text in Section III.A: 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient' and 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation'. The 'safety-critical functionality' and 'low-latency communication' requirements are also explicitly mentioned in Section III.A. The extraction is specific, detailed, and well-grounded.

The extraction accurately identifies the three core services defined in Section III.A: 'Data Monitoring', 'Computation Offloading', and 'Remote Management'. The details for Data Monitoring match the text: 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports' and display 'real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks'. The Computation Offloading description correctly states that the 'ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed... sending the desired speed back' and is done to alleviate 'computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board hardware' via 'cloud-based resources' (implied by 'better-suited equipment'). Remote Management capabilities ('enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and send emergency braking') and the target users ('operators and developers') are also precisely aligned with the source text.

The list of hardware and software components is complete and matches the source text exactly. It includes specific models like the 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', and tools like 'ROS2' and 'MATLAB/Simulink', along with their intended uses. Quotes: 'NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano', 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The description correctly identifies the three entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical Twin), their deployment on separate hardware components, and the specific functions handled by each. It also accurately notes the deviation from ISO 23247 RA regarding the Physical Twin Entity replacing the OME and the impact on functionality ownership. Supported by quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'Data pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the Physical Twin Entity.', 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA.'

The extraction provides a comprehensive summary of the architecture, correctly stating it is based on ISO 23247 RA, implemented via ROS2 packages and nodes. It accurately details the composition of the entities and notes the specific deviation where the Physical Twin Entity replaces the standard OME. Supported by quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes', 'Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific functionality.', 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity... SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (DTS), mapping components to ISO 23247 entities and discussing communication between internal entities (User, Digital Twin, Physical). It does not mention integration with external systems, interoperability standards for external data exchange, or integration patterns with other enterprise systems. Therefore, the assessment is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the data flow from physical sensors (LiDAR, vehicle state) through processing components (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, Data Translation) to visualization. It correctly identifies the communication methods and specific data transformations mentioned in the text. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds', 'Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The extraction correctly traces the control path from the ACC Controlling FE through the Data Translation component to the physical actuators (Motor, Steering Servo). It correctly notes the absence of validation and authentication mechanisms in the text. Quotes: 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'Data Translation component serializes data'.

The extraction correctly identifies the protocols used: ROS2 for internal software communication and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. It correctly identifies that specific infrastructure details like bandwidth and security are not present. Quotes: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network', 'inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'.

The characteristic definition requires details on hosting platforms (cloud/edge), specific computational requirements, and scalability mechanisms. The source text mentions storing data on a 'device external to the vehicle' and that entities are 'deployed on a different hardware component', but does not specify the hosting platform type, computational requirements, or scalability models. Thus, stating the information is 'Not in Document' is accurate relative to the required depth. Quotes: 'store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle', 'each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task

does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source documents focus on the architecture, implementation, and case study of an Adaptive Cruise Control Digital Twin. While lifecycle management is mentioned briefly in the context of related work ('DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management tool'), the text does not describe the specific lifecycle phases, development stages, transition management, or governance approaches of the Digital Twin itself. Therefore, the assertion that this is not in the document is correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The source text details the architectural design, requirements, and implementation of the DT system (Section IV), including mapping components to ISO 23247. However, it does not discuss specific engineering methodologies for the twinning process, quality assurance practices, version control, or evolution management strategies beyond the immediate case study development. The absence of these specific process and evolution details justifies the 'Not in Document' claim.

The extraction accurately identifies the three key services—Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, and Remote Management—and the user interface capabilities described in Section III.A and Requirements R4/R5. Quotes: 'develop the following three services... 1) Data Monitoring... 2) Computation Offloading... 3) Remote Management'; 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports'; 'enable the management and operation of ACC remotely'.

The extraction accurately describes the ISO 23247 standard's four parts, the reference architecture entities (OME, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), and the mention of OPC UA for interoperability. This is fully supported by the source text. Quotes include: 'The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4) Information exchange', 'The Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)... The Data Collection and Device Control Entity... The Digital Twin Entity... The User Entity...', and 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA)... to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE'.
